text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Priya Menon  
Harley Street Family Practice  
12 Harley Street, London W1G 9PG  

Dear Dr Priya Menon,  

Sarah Bennett, DOB: 04-07-1984, Hospital No: U914726 NHS No. 1038475629  
Flat 5, 28 Kingsway, London WC2B 6LE 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Long-standing seizure disorder with fluctuating control; high-frequency international business travel with irregular sleep and time zones; migraine without aura; mild anxiety symptoms related to travel logistics; phone-based follow-up acceptable and frequently used; non-smoker; minimal alcohol. 

Medication: Levetiracetam 1500 mg twice daily.  
Lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily.  
Propranolol 20 mg as needed for performance anxiety during presentations.  
Rizatriptan 10 mg as needed for migraine. 

I reviewed her today by scheduled telephone consultation, as she is currently in transit between London and Singapore for client meetings. The call quality was intermittently distorted (notes contain characters Ã‚Â· ï¿½ â€ due to VoIP artefacts). She reports that work requires frequent crossings of multiple time zones, with short layovers and irregular sleep windows; she often sleeps 3–4 hours at variable local times and shifts her morning dose forward for early flights. She uses phone alarms to anchor dosing but occasionally takes the evening dose 2–3 hours later than planned when meetings overrun. Appetite is intact. No recent intercurrent illness. She denies head injury, new neurological deficit, or recent hospital admissions. Mood is mostly stable; she describes anticipatory stress around red-eye flights and critical presentations but no panic attacks. 

Seizures: Over the last six weeks, coinciding with an intensified travel schedule, she has experienced clusters of jumps almost 1 per day, compared to the prior pattern of just a few episodes each month. She links the increase to sleep restriction and circadian disruption. No loss of awareness described. No tongue bite or urinary incontinence. Recovery is immediate and she resumes activity. There have been no injuries. She has kept a basic electronic log on her phone, though time-stamps reflect local time in different countries. She has not missed doses but acknowledges variable timing. No new triggers apart from sleep and jet lag. Caffeine intake is moderate; she avoids alcohol on flight days. 

Examination: Not performed today as this was a phone review. In prior clinic her neurological examination was unremarkable, with normal gait and coordination. 

Investigations: Last routine bloods (U&Es, LFTs, FBC) six months ago were within reference ranges. No recent antiseizure drug levels measured. MRI brain from 2021 unchanged from baseline. 

Plan: 
- No immediate change to antiseizure medication today while we clarify dose timing around long-haul flights. We discussed consolidating a stable 12-hour dosing anchor referenced to UK time for the next month, using dual alarms to minimise drift. 
- Arrange repeat bloods (U&Es, LFTs, FBC, vitamin D) at her GP surgery within 2 weeks; we will send forms electronically. 
- Provide a brief written plan for dosing during time-zone changes and a travel letter for airport security. 
- Continue phone-based follow-up given her schedule; we will review in 4 weeks by video if possible. She will email her seizure log (noting local time and city) for correlation. 
- Safety counselled regarding sleep optimisation on flights where feasible, hydration, and avoiding missed doses; she will inform us urgently if events evolve or injuries occur. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Over the last six weeks, coinciding with an intensified travel schedule, she has experienced clusters of jumps almost 1 per day
she has experienced clusters of jumps almost 1 per day, compared to the prior pattern of just a few episodes each month

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states the recent seizure frequency: "Over the last six weeks ... she has experienced clusters of jumps almost 1 per day," indicating a current rate close to daily. No other higher frequency is provided. The prior pattern was lower (a few per month), but the rule says pick the highest current frequency. Therefore, normalize to '1 per day'.
